summary: I was born in Okayama Prefecture in January 1900. Although we lived in the countryside, all the children went to school in the city. My mother attended a teachers' college there before she had to quit to get married. It was a small village, and life was quiet with not much happening during my childhood.

One of my father's relatives attended Doshisha College, but my father himself could not go because he was the only son. This influenced my father to allow all his children to attend school if they wanted. So, education was important in our family, even though my father did not have much education himself.

I learned about Ninomiya Sontoku when I was young, but I was not very impressed by him. It was part of my early education, but he did not leave a strong impression on me. Still, his teachings were known in my community.

My family practiced Buddhism, specifically the Koyasan sect. My parents were not very ardent believers, but my grandmother was devoted. Buddhism was part of our religious background growing up in Okayama.

Our family followed Buddhism of the Koyasan sect. While my parents were not very strict about it, my grandmother was a devout follower. This religious tradition was part of my upbringing in Okayama Prefecture.

My second oldest brother worked for Mitsu Bussan and traveled to Australia to buy wool. Unfortunately, he has passed away, but his work took him abroad and was important for our family. Australia was a place connected to my brother’s career.

My oldest brother graduated from Bunka University and later worked for the Ministry of Agriculture. His education there helped him secure a government position, which was a source of pride for our family. Bunka Gakuen University played a role in shaping his career.

My second oldest brother worked for Mitsu Bussan, which is Mitsubishi Corporation, after graduating from college. He was involved in international trade, including trips to Australia to buy wool. His career with Mitsubishi was significant in our family history.

When we came to America, we landed first in Hawaii. One of Itano’s acquaintances treated us with bonito sashimi, which was very delicious and made me feel better after the seasickness. Hawaii was our first stop before arriving on the mainland.

In Hawaii, an acquaintance of my husband treated us with bonito sashimi. It was very delicious and helped me feel better after being seasick on the ship. That was my first experience tasting bonito in America.

We arrived in San Francisco after our trip from Japan and Hawaii. We stayed with friends there before moving on. I worked as a schoolgirl in Burlingame near San Francisco and took English classes at a Buddhist church in the city. San Francisco was my first home in America.
We did not go to Angel Island when we arrived in America. Only people who came alone or had diseases were sent there. Since I came with my husband, we were not processed there.

After working as a schoolgirl, my husband went to farm in Freeport, California, and he asked me to join him. I agreed and we moved to the country there. It was a new phase of our life in America, away from the city.

By Armistice Day, we were living in Sacramento, California. My husband farmed there, growing various crops like tomatoes, onions, potatoes, and corn, though he was not a very good farmer. I stayed home and cooked for about four to five people, including laborers we hired.

Although not directly mentioned in my story, my family valued education highly, and some relatives attended prestigious institutions. For example, my father's relative attended Doshisha College, and my brothers pursued higher education. The University of California, Berkeley, was a well-known institution in the area where I lived after coming to America.

I heard that some picture brides who came to America sought help from organizations like the Salvation Army, but I do not recall much about the Nihonjin-kai. It was one of the community organizations for Japanese immigrants, but I was not directly involved with it.

Some picture brides who came to America without a place to go sought help from the Salvation Army. I knew of this because some brides ended up there after arriving. It was a support organization for those in need among the Japanese immigrant community.

The Great Depression was not directly mentioned in my story, but I know that many Japanese immigrants faced hardships during that time. Some husbands had trouble finding work, and some even returned to Japan because of economic difficulties. My husband worked hard as a farmer despite the challenges.
